title: Reading Other People's Code
intro: You just joined a new team and need to fix a bug in code you've never seen before. The original developer left the company. No documentation. Think of it like being a detective at a crime scene -- you follow clues and trace evidence instead of reading every document in the building. Let's practice the skill of reading unfamiliar code.

teach.title: Reading Code = Exploring a New City
teach.body: When you visit a new city, you don't read every street sign in order. You get a map, find the landmarks, and navigate from there. **Reading unfamiliar code** works the same way -- start with the big picture (file structure, main entry point), identify landmarks (key functions, data flow), then zoom into the specific area you need.

teach.title: Reading Code = Being a Detective at a Crime Scene
teach.body: A detective doesn't read every document in a suspect's house. They look for clues: the entry point (the front door), trace the evidence (fingerprints and footprints), and zero in on the relevant room. **Reading unfamiliar code is detective work** -- you follow the data trail from where the problem appears back to where it originates, ignoring everything that isn't connected to your case.

teach.title: The Strategy for Reading Unfamiliar Code
teach.body:
Follow this order when diving into a new codebase:

1. **File structure** -- Look at folder names to understand the project's shape
2. **Entry point** -- Find where the app starts (index.js, App.js, main.py)
3. **Data flow** -- Trace how data moves: where it comes from, where it's transformed, where it's displayed
4. **The specific area** -- Once you understand the big picture, zoom into the file relevant to your task

teach.title: Clues in File Structure
teach.code:
src/
  components/     <- UI pieces
    Header.jsx
    ProductCard.jsx
  hooks/          <- Custom React hooks
    useAuth.js
    useProducts.js
  api/            <- Server communication
    auth.js
    products.js
  utils/          <- Helper functions
    formatPrice.js
    validateEmail.js
teach.annotation: Even without reading any code, this file structure tells you a lot: the app has products and authentication, the UI is in components/, data fetching is in api/, and shared logic is in utils/.

teach.body: Being able to read other people's code unlocks a whole category of paid work: fixing, extending, and maintaining existing apps. Most clients don't need a new app built from scratch -- they need someone who can jump into their codebase and improve it. That's recurring revenue waiting to happen.

Reading unfamiliar code is a skill, not a talent. Start with the file structure, find the entry point, trace the data flow, then zoom into the specific area you need. Don't try to understand everything at once.

start.narrative:
Bug report: 'Users see the wrong price on the product detail page. The price shows $0 instead of the actual price.'

You've never seen this codebase before. The project has this structure:

src/
  components/ (Header, ProductList, ProductDetail, Cart)
  hooks/ (useAuth, useProducts, useCart)
  api/ (auth.js, products.js, orders.js)
  utils/ (formatPrice.js, validators.js)

Where do you start?

choices.text: Open ProductDetail component since that's where the bug is visible
Open api/products.js to check if the API returns the right price
Start at App.js and read through the entire codebase

open-detail.narrative:
You open ProductDetail.jsx and see: const { product } = useProducts(id); return

{product.name}

{formatPrice(product.price)}

The component uses useProducts() hook and formatPrice() utility. The data flow is: API -> hook -> component -> formatter. Where do you go next to find the $0?

choices.text: Walk the data backward: hook first, then API, then formatter -- closest layer to the symptom
Check formatPrice() in utils/ first because it touches the value last
Open every related file at once and skim them in parallel

open-api.narrative: api/products.js fetches from /api/products/:id and returns the raw response. It looks correct. But the bug might be between the API response and what the component displays. You realize you skipped a layer.

choices.text: Go back to the symptom -- open the component first to see what it actually consumes

read-all.narrative: 30 minutes later, you've read 400 lines of unrelated code about authentication and cart logic. You still haven't looked at anything related to product prices. Reading everything is too slow when you have a specific bug to find.

choices.text: Focus on the product detail page where the bug actually appears
The value is in cents (integers like 2999 for $29.99) -- a unit conversion is missing somewhere
The name is just legacy -- I'll trust the value as-is and move on
Hard to say -- need to log it to see

verify-data.narrative: You confirm at runtime: a normal product has price_cents = 2999 ($29.99). A newly-created product without a price has price_cents = null, and the || 0 fallback makes it become 0. The bug-reporting user happens to be looking at one of those new products. So there are TWO problems: the unit mismatch AND the silent fallback. How do you fix it?

choices.text: Convert cents to dollars AND surface missing prices: price: data.price_cents == null ? null : data.price_cents / 100, then render 'Price unavailable' when null
Just convert cents to dollars: price: (data.price_cents || 0) / 100
Just remove the || 0 fallback and let null crash

verify-fix.narrative: Before you ship, how do you verify the fix and protect against regressions?

choices.text: Reproduce the original bug locally with a null-price product, confirm it now shows 'Price unavailable', then add a unit test for both the cents-conversion and the null case
Eyeball the change in the dev environment with one normal product and call it done
Push to production and let real users tell us if it broke anything else

end-good.narrative: Test passes, the original repro now shows 'Price unavailable' for the null case and $29.99 for normal products. You traced the bug from the symptom backward through the component -> hook -> API, found the unit mismatch hidden by the fallback, and locked the fix in with a test. Total time: under an hour in code you'd never seen.
end-good.feedback: This is the experienced developer's playbook for unfamiliar code: start at the symptom, walk the data backward, read field names as clues (price_cents tells you the unit), and lock the fix in with a test so the next reader inherits both the fix AND the proof. The code didn't need to be familiar -- just methodically followed.

end-mediocre.narrative: The eyeball test on a normal product shows $29.99 instead of $0 -- looks fixed! You ship. Two days later, QA reports that the 'Price unavailable' wording is missing for null-price products: the page just shows '$0.00' again, because you only fixed the conversion, not the fallback path. You're back in this code for a second debugging round.
end-mediocre.feedback: You found the right area but verified only the happy path. When you spot two problems (unit mismatch AND silent fallback), you have to test both. A 30-second test for the null case would have caught the partial fix before it shipped.

end-weak.narrative: Production users now see -- in some cases -- a JavaScript crash on the product page (null.toFixed). Support tickets pile up overnight. The on-call engineer rolls back your change and the original $0 bug returns. You broke a working-but-buggy page into a fully broken page.
end-weak.feedback: Shipping unverified changes to unfamiliar code is the highest-risk move you can make. The whole point of reading other people's code methodically is to build enough understanding to know what to test. Skipping verification turns careful detective work into a coin flip.
